content.text: What Guardrails Are and Why They Are Mandatory

Chaos experiments are controlled failures. Without guardrails, they become uncontrolled failures. A guardrail is a mechanism that prevents a chaos experiment from exceeding its intended scope — either by aborting the experiment automatically when a threshold is crossed, or by preventing the experiment from starting at all if preconditions are not met. Guardrails are not optional safety theater; they are the technical boundary that separates planned learning from accidental production incidents.

GenAI services have two properties that make guardrails especially critical. First, a safety classifier outage is a high-severity data-safety event regardless of whether it was induced by chaos or a real failure — the consequences of allowing output through an absent classifier are the same either way. Second, provider API retry storms can generate thousands of tokens per minute in costs; a runaway retry loop during a chaos experiment can produce five-figure billing spikes in minutes.

attrs.alt: Failure injection guardrail architecture: pre-flight checks gate experiment start, live monitors trigger automated abort, rollback restores steady state, blast radius limits scope, and approval workflow governs who can run what in production

content.text: Pre-Flight Checks: Gating Experiment Start

No experiment starts without passing all pre-flight checks. The checks are fast (sub-second) and automated; a human cannot bypass them by accident. If any check fails, the experiment controller emits a structured rejection event and halts.
Check
How to measure
Block condition
Steady-state probe
HTTP GET /metrics; compare to baseline
Error rate > 0.5% or p99 > 110% of baseline
Active incident check
PagerDuty API: list_incidents?status=triggered
Any open P1/P2 incident in this service
Maintenance window
Ops calendar API or ConfigMap field
Current time is inside any maintenance window
Traffic volume
Request rate vs. daily P50 traffic
Current rate < 30% of typical (too quiet to get signal)
Peak-hours exclusion
Wall-clock hour in service config
Local time 09:00–18:00 Monday–Friday (configurable)
Change ticket
Jira API: check ticket status field
Ticket not in Approved state
Experiment lock
Redis SETNX chaos:lock:<service>
Another experiment already running on this service
Automated Abort: Continuous Monitoring During Injection

Once injection starts, a dedicated monitor polls key metrics every 5 seconds. When any abort threshold is crossed, the monitor triggers the abort sequence without waiting for human attention. The sequence runs in under 10 seconds:
Remove the chaos artifact (delete the Toxiproxy toxic or the LitmusChaos ChaosEngine resource).
Page the on-call engineer with the abort reason and metric snapshot.
Emit a structured abort event to the experiment tracking system.
Block the experiment controller from starting another experiment on this service for 60 minutes.
Re-run the steady-state probe; wait for metrics to normalize before closing the experiment record.

import asyncio
import time
import httpx
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Callable, Awaitable, Optional
from enum import Enum

class AbortReason(Enum):
    ERROR_RATE_HIGH    = "error_rate_exceeded_threshold"
    LATENCY_HIGH       = "p99_latency_exceeded_3x_baseline"
    CLASSIFIER_FAIL_OPEN = "classifier_block_rate_below_minimum"
    TOKEN_SPEND_HIGH   = "token_spend_rate_exceeded_2x"
    PII_UNREACHABLE    = "pii_detector_unreachable"
    TIMEOUT            = "experiment_duration_exceeded"
    MANUAL             = "manual_abort"

@dataclass
class AbortEvent:
    reason: AbortReason
    metric_snapshot: dict
    elapsed_s: float
    timestamp: float = field(default_factory=lambda: 0.0)  # set by monitor


class ExperimentMonitor:
    def __init__(
        self,
        gateway_url: str,
        classifier_url: str,
        pagerduty_url: str,
        baseline: dict,                     # {"error_rate_pct": 0.4, "p99_ms": 1500, ...}
        rollback_fn: Callable[[], Awaitable[None]],
        max_duration_s: int = 600,
        poll_interval_s: float = 5.0,
    ):
        self.gateway_url = gateway_url
        self.classifier_url = classifier_url
        self.pagerduty_url = pagerduty_url
        self.baseline = baseline
        self.rollback_fn = rollback_fn
        self.max_duration_s = max_duration_s
        self.poll_interval_s = poll_interval_s
        self.abort_event: Optional[AbortEvent] = None

    async def run(self) -> Optional[AbortEvent]:
        start = time.monotonic()
        while True:
            elapsed = time.monotonic() - start
            if elapsed >= self.max_duration_s:
                await self._trigger_abort(AbortReason.TIMEOUT, elapsed, {})
                return self.abort_event

            snapshot = await self._sample_metrics()
            reason = self._evaluate(snapshot)
            if reason:
                await self._trigger_abort(reason, elapsed, snapshot)
                return self.abort_event

            await asyncio.sleep(self.poll_interval_s)

    async def _sample_metrics(self) -> dict:
        async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=3.0) as client:
            gw = await client.get(f"{self.gateway_url}/metrics/current")
            gw_data = gw.json()
            try:
                clf = await client.get(f"{self.classifier_url}/health")
                clf_ok = clf.status_code == 200
            except Exception:
                clf_ok = False
        return {
            "error_rate_pct": gw_data.get("error_rate_pct", 0),
            "p99_ms": gw_data.get("p99_latency_ms", 0),
            "block_rate_pct": gw_data.get("classifier_block_rate_pct", 0),
            "token_rate": gw_data.get("token_spend_per_min", 0),
            "classifier_reachable": clf_ok,
        }

    def _evaluate(self, s: dict) -> Optional[AbortReason]:
        if s["error_rate_pct"] > self.baseline["error_rate_pct"] * 10:
            return AbortReason.ERROR_RATE_HIGH
        if s["p99_ms"] > self.baseline["p99_ms"] * 3:
            return AbortReason.LATENCY_HIGH
        if s["block_rate_pct"] < self.baseline.get("block_rate_pct", 2.0) * 0.5:
            return AbortReason.CLASSIFIER_FAIL_OPEN
        if s["token_rate"] > self.baseline.get("token_rate", 1000) * 2:
            return AbortReason.TOKEN_SPEND_HIGH
        if not s["classifier_reachable"]:
            return AbortReason.PII_UNREACHABLE
        return None

    async def _trigger_abort(self, reason: AbortReason, elapsed_s: float, snapshot: dict) -> None:
        self.abort_event = AbortEvent(reason=reason, metric_snapshot=snapshot, elapsed_s=elapsed_s)
        # 1. Remove chaos
        await self.rollback_fn()
        # 2. Page on-call
        async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=5.0) as client:
            await client.post(self.pagerduty_url, json={
                "routing_key": "PAGERDUTY_ROUTING_KEY",
                "event_action": "trigger",
                "payload": {
                    "summary": f"[CHAOS ABORTED] {reason.value} after {elapsed_s:.0f}s",
                    "severity": "warning",
                    "custom_details": snapshot,
                },
            })
        print(f"[chaos] ABORTED: {reason.value} at {elapsed_s:.1f}s")
Blast Radius Limiting

Blast radius is the maximum scope of harm the experiment can cause. Reducing blast radius is the primary tool for making production experiments safe enough to run during off-peak hours without a full maintenance window:
Label-based pod targeting:
annotate only the pods that participate in chaos experiments with
chaos-eligible: true
. The chaos controller uses a label selector to restrict its targets; system pods, PVCs, and StatefulSets are never in scope.
Traffic percentage cap:

route at most 10% of production traffic through the chaos-eligible path using weighted routing in the load balancer or service mesh. If the experiment causes those requests to fail, the maximum user impact is 10%.
Experiment duration hard cap:

the chaos controller has a hard maximum duration (typically 10 minutes) after which it removes the fault unconditionally, even if the experiment has not reached its planned end time. This is a separate timer from the experiment window.
Service-level lock:
a distributed lock prevents two experiments from running on the same service simultaneously. The lock has a TTL so a crashed controller never permanently blocks future experiments.
Safety path exclusion:
the safety classifier, PII detector, and audit log writer are permanently excluded from production chaos. These paths can only be tested in isolated staging namespaces.

# Istio VirtualService: route 10% of traffic through chaos-eligible deployment
# The remaining 90% goes to the stable deployment, unaffected by any chaos
apiVersion: networking.istio.io/v1beta1
kind: VirtualService
metadata:
  name: llm-gateway-chaos-split
  namespace: llm-serving
spec:
  hosts:
    - llm-gateway
  http:
    - match:
        - headers:
            x-chaos-eligible:
              exact: "true"
      route:
        - destination:
            host: llm-gateway-chaos
            port:
              number: 8080
          weight: 100
    - route:
        - destination:
            host: llm-gateway-stable
            port:
              number: 8080
          weight: 90
        - destination:
            host: llm-gateway-chaos
            port:
              number: 8080
          weight: 10
---
# Kubernetes annotation on chaos-eligible pods only
# Non-eligible pods are never targeted by chaos controllers
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: llm-gateway-chaos
  namespace: llm-serving
spec:
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: llm-gateway
        chaos-eligible: "true"      # only pods with this label are targeted
      annotations:
        chaos.company.com/blast-radius: "10-percent-traffic"
        chaos.company.com/safety-path: "false"
Rollback Verification

Rollback is not complete until the system is confirmed healthy — not just until the chaos artifact is removed. A chaos experiment that removes a toxic but leaves the service in a degraded state has transferred the failure from the experiment to the production environment. Rollback verification requires a separate steady-state probe that blocks experiment closure until it passes:

import asyncio
import time
import httpx
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class RollbackResult:
    recovery_s: float              # seconds from rollback to steady state restored
    steady_state_passed: bool
    failure_reason: Optional[str]  # set if recovery timed out

async def verify_rollback(
    gateway_url: str,
    baseline: dict,
    timeout_s: int = 300,
    poll_s: float = 10.0,
) -> RollbackResult:
    start = time.monotonic()
    failure_reason = None

    while True:
        elapsed = time.monotonic() - start
        if elapsed > timeout_s:
            failure_reason = f"system did not recover within {timeout_s}s after chaos removal"
            break

        try:
            async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=5.0) as client:
                r = await client.get(f"{gateway_url}/metrics/current")
                m = r.json()
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"[rollback] metrics probe failed: {e} ({elapsed:.0f}s elapsed)")
            await asyncio.sleep(poll_s)
            continue

        error_ok = m["error_rate_pct"] <= baseline["error_rate_pct"] * 1.1
        p99_ok   = m["p99_latency_ms"] <= baseline["p99_ms"] * 1.1

        if error_ok and p99_ok:
            return RollbackResult(
                recovery_s=elapsed,
                steady_state_passed=True,
                failure_reason=None,
            )

        print(
            f"[rollback] waiting for recovery: "
            f"err={m['error_rate_pct']:.2f}% (limit={baseline['error_rate_pct']*1.1:.2f}%) "
            f"p99={m['p99_latency_ms']}ms (limit={baseline['p99_ms']*1.1:.0f}ms) "
            f"[{elapsed:.0f}s]"
        )
        await asyncio.sleep(poll_s)

    return RollbackResult(
        recovery_s=time.monotonic() - start,
        steady_state_passed=False,
        failure_reason=failure_reason,
    )
What Guardrails Cannot Protect Against
Guardrails reduce but do not eliminate risk. Teams that understand the limits avoid false confidence:
Cascading failures faster than the poll interval:

if a 5-second poll misses a 3-second error spike that triggers a database connection pool exhaustion, the guardrail fires too late. Reduce poll interval for high-risk paths, but accept that some fast cascades will not be caught.
Abort execution failures:

if the chaos controller crashes at the moment of abort, the rollback function never runs. The experiment duration hard cap is the second line of defense here — it eventually removes the fault when its TTL expires.
Metrics pipeline lag:

Prometheus scrape intervals are typically 15–30 seconds. A guardrail that reads error rate from Prometheus may not see a rate spike for up to 30 seconds after it starts. Use push-based metrics (StatsD, OpenTelemetry direct export) for guardrail probes to minimize lag.
Unknown unknowns:

a guardrail monitors the metrics you thought to include. A blast radius that creates symptoms in a service you did not monitor will not trigger an abort. This is why on-call engineers are notified at experiment start and remain reachable for the full injection window.
Summary

Failure injection guardrails are the boundary between controlled experimentation and accidental incidents. Pre-flight checks prevent experiments from starting in degraded conditions. Continuous monitoring with automated abort removes the fault faster than any human can respond. Blast radius limiting caps the scope of harm to a fraction of production traffic. Rollback verification ensures the experiment leaves the system no worse than it found it.

For GenAI specifically, two guardrail properties are non-negotiable: safety-critical paths (classifier, PII detector) must be permanently excluded from production chaos regardless of who approves the experiment, and the automated abort must trigger on classifier fail-open before a single unsafe completion reaches a user. No human in an approval workflow moves fast enough to prevent that outcome — the guardrail must.
